In this section, we will derive a useful formula known as Cauchy's re formula, which combines together powetful techniques from earlier seet The applications of this formula will be explored throughout the cha Let us start by reviewing a few results from Laurent series. Suppose $f$ has an isolated singularity at 20 . We know from Theorem 1, Section that $f$ has a Laurent series in an annulus around $2_{0}$ : for $0<|2-20|<$
$$
f(z)=\cdots+\frac{a_{-2}}{\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}}+\frac{a_{-1}}{z-z_{0}}+a_{0}+a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)+a_{2}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}+\cdots
$$

Могениет.
$$
a_{-1}=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \int_{C_{r}\left(z_{0}\right)} f(z) d z,
$$
where $C_{r}\left(z_{0}\right)$ is any positively oriented circle in the annulus $0<\left|z-z_{0}\right|$ The coefficient $a_{-1}$ is called the residue of $f$ at $z_{0}$ and is denote Res $\left(f . z_{0}\right)$ or simply Res $\left(z_{0}\right)$ when there is no risk of confusing the fun $f$. With the concept of residue in hand, we can state our main result.
COREM 1 RESIDUE HEOREM
let $C$ be a simple closed positively oriented path. Suppose that $f$ is an inside and on $C$, except at finitely many isolated singularities $z_{1}$, $z_{2}$ inside C. Then
$$
\int_{C} f(z) d z=2 \pi i \sum_{j=1}^{n} \operatorname{Res}\left(z_{j}\right)
$$

Proof Take amall circles $C_{r,}\left(z_{j}\right)(j=1,2, \ldots, n)$ that do not intersec other and are contained in the interior of $C$ (Figure 1). Apply Cauchy's i theoren for multiply connected regions (Theorem 6, Section 3.4) and get
$$
\int_{C} f(z) d z=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \int_{C,} f(z) d z=2 \pi i \sum_{j=1}^{n} \operatorname{Res}\left(z_{j}\right)
$$

where the last equality follows from (1). So (2) holds.
An analog of Theorem 1 holds if $C$ is negatively oriented; in this pick up a negative sign on the right side of (2).

The residue theorem has abounding applications to the evaluation sorts of integrals. Our first example illustrates the basic ideas behi process.
